author: @obliograce3551
text: I think I missed something. You showed the paint and said it was great to use and a real break through ... then you never used the paint? I appreciate the tear downs but, I was hoping for some ideas on building something for off grid use in a home or a RV (Caravan to the British). I love to do what I call "MacGyver" things, a name I made up (or heard somewhere) after a television series called "MacGyver" where  every week the guy would build something important out of seemly random parts, often to a ludicrous degree (here’s a box of spare parts, I am now going to build an airplane to get away from the bad guys). I wasn't a fan of the show at the time, but in the world of making something out of spare parts, saying that I am going to “MacGyver" something by taking spare parts out of old electronic devices and hobbling together something new to use sounds more positively inventive than does "Frankenstein" Same idea I suppose but with mechanical parts, not with people.

author: @annettek9375
text: Hello i wanted to ask if i can turn a soldering iron in to a element to heat up sand with to make a heater for my balcony? I live in sweden and I need a heater that heats well and constant and runns on very low watts. I have a solarpanel 100 watt, controller, and a car battery 60 AH  and a converter constant power 2500w ( 5000w peak) and need a heater solution that i can make my self and will not run down the battery to zero everyday and leaves juice in the battery to run 44 watt lights 10 h/ day. Could you help me? Realy love your videos!

author: @Information_Seeker
text: a roughly 1sq meter diy solar air heating panel could be built about 250$, and be easily changed to a cooler for summer. if it was 100% efficient that'd be ~1300 watts heating for just the cost of running a fan, and that's before you consider the possibility of making a larger or multiple panels

text: That is why the phrase "keep is simple" still applies to this day. Complexity add cost with diminishing returns. It also adds failure points that could be avoided.

author: @hubrisnaut
text:
Reference: The paint Robert has here and in other videos is "Rustin's G Therm Thermal Heating Paint". It's a UK brand. The first ever to come to market, I believe. Similar radiant paint brands can be found now here in the US. 
Fascinating possibilities.

author: @velcroman11
text: It the days when toasters etc where repairable 1950s-60s.  If the toasting/heating wires broke I would pull the heating card out and “twist” the broken wires together and put it back together.  I would do the same with the wire heater in the electric jug.  End result super fast toaster and super fast kettle.  The super fast toaster cooked the surface of the bread very fast leaving the inside soft, really nice toast.

author: @travismoore7849
text: One Winter I tried to use some steel mesh or a steel scrubbing pad in the top of a hurricane lantern. But I don't think it worked too well. But the thermal mass heater did warm the room that had a candle under a steel pot of water. And it used a few larger candles and the steel pot was in a larger deeper steel pot. I could knock a bit of the chill out of my room.

replies.author: @travismoore7849
replies.text: Also it did have to have enough room for air to circulate.

author: @PsiQ
text:
For cheap/free lower voltage heatings i always recommend old car lights.
front ones had 60 + 75W .. brake+ indicator lights tend to not break that easily, especially if you underrun them.. I think they go from around 5W to 15W depending on the type of light..
and then you can just scale them in a row to the voltage you have.

author: @littleworkshopofhorrors2395
text: They have made that brand of fan heater even cheaper than before by leaving out the other switch in the base that would  turn the power off if it fell over.

author: @ryandavis7593
text:
As a locomotive electrician I deal with safety issues all of the time. The number one thing is to know what you are touching. All of our machines are forty years old or older. One is turning seventy this upcoming year. Because of their ages they often need to be upgraded with more modern parts as the old parts have outlived their expected lifespan by double or more. I have zero fear in taking things apart and making these modifications. Experience and education have made this perfectly safe. 

What’s interesting is the fact that I learned nearly everything I needed to know keeping my model trains running before I was ten. All the principles are the same even though the complexity is greater. 
Experimentation is the greatest teacher.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
What about coating fiberglass matt. Use copper plate wnd copper rivets and glue the copper to the fiber glass matt with thr carbon ink that is coating the woven fiberglass matt. Then possibly look into coating it all with enamel like used on copper coiling wire, or a figh temperature silicone, glue a thermal protection device to it. Wiring it in of course to limit the temp to below the boiling of water.. 180° f or there about,  and it used along with a temp cintrol and possibly a dc boost converter . Sew it on to gloves, in clothing, in jackers, jeans, or chapps, sox , boots,  or even use nichrome wire sround 26-28gsuge weave it through the cloth, say a. Sock use another thin sock  sew them together quilting thrm tiborotect the wearer. Of course coat the wire with enamel, or silicone or some other temperature and moisture resistant insulator. Use a 20 ah lifepo4 batter, set it up with a bimetallic switch to power up a dc boost converter to boost it to around 
32- 36v  to fight the resistance, after it is up to heat using thin leather to hold in thr heat would be a awesome way to store some heat, a thermal mass, especially a sleeping bag made this way with about 24'-32' of 28 gauge nichrome wire wiven through a cloth coated with conductive ink. And backed or sanwiched between  thin leather  and sewn 
Into a sleeping bag or a blanket, it could keep a person warm even in a roomsround 30°- 35°f even colder, pull about 12v, 15 amps , and swetup with a pwm controller, using frequency to regulate temp along eith actualbon off controllers, the pwm running around 20 hz, to around 400hz to eliminate hot spots while getting up to temperature with it pulsing at 20 or even 10hz, while being on for 4-5 minutes out of 15-20. Thr battery should last for a couple days it could be connected to 50-60 watt pv pannel, to charge daily, if a person is walking and camping . Build a bouncing gravity charger use a serpent coil around 26 ga, slider about 8' long, a stater about 12" long, and 6"wide ,the slider fitting inside being 5.5"-,5.7" wide, and having bar magnets about 4-6mm wide and enough to equal 5.5-5.7"wide. Running thr length of the slider,  attaching to the backpack at te top and each dide on it's bottom . With s coated cable attaching to the bottom of the slider. A spring on the top. With a weight on the bottom of the 6" +/- cable.set the spring and weight so it will bounce while walking the slider sliding up and down generating power . The thin long n52 magnets and possibly 2-4 phase serpent coil linear generator with UpTo 14- near 16" of travel. The thin magne help in the simulation of a high rpm generator wrapping the coil around a tiny, but magnet fitting  iron core. It should be equal to a generator spinning 1200rpm +/- s couple hundred rpm. And it can be used manually, possibly making an attachment bar, with a leve and handle, looking similar to a framing swuare, anf a level with a bicycle hand grip on it,  breaking down , fokding up into a 2-3" wide 12-14" long pouch with a similar wider pouch the generator can fit inside, and fold at the divider.  To make it more compact.  The weight can be a survival kit with a knife , matches, and alot more, a thick 3"-4"steel pipe, about 6"-8"long.  Inside a rubbery plastic cover  with a screw on lid, to seal out water. It being removable, to hold water, while the steel part can boil the water, both having waterproof lids. The water can be boiled then sat in the cold water to cool off, and the cover used to hold mire wate, or wait to hold the cool water. And boil more water.  The contents being held inside another similar container inside. So they do not rattle loudly!  The contents also inside a thick plastic bag. So they can be kept completely separate while being protected.  It could be a actual thing people can buy,  it would not be expensive to build. And could use a dc to DC buck boost. To output 5v usb,12-13.8v hold a 4-6 farad cap bank to store power. So the dc converter isnt on and off constantly. And ut doesnit stop just because you paused for a couple seconds. Hopefully it would be enough to keep the charger going for 2-5 minutes depending on load. Great for camping, may be a micro version with a harness for dogs.   Mount yo the vest like harness, generator on one side, a bank of super caps on the other, with the option of one per side. Charge the like 50 farad capacitor, or possibly lifepo4 battery a 4ah or so. With a light that shows the battery or caps are charged.  No dangerous flammable batteries, no lmn lipo  battery , that  could harm the animals if it malfunctioned or was damaged. 3-5 ah is plenty to power a light, charge a phone, add charge to a laptop, charge a radio. And a dog running around can produce a fair amount if power if harnessed. Just like people!!

text: I once made some 12 volt heaters from power resistors inside an aluminium box filled with sand. They were safe and kept some pet guinea pigs warm.

author: @grunwode
text: Seems like the linear thermal fuse in the fan motor would go out pretty early.  On my old space heater, the tip over switch is attached to the bimetallic thermostat, but I imagine they could be placed almost anywhere.

author: @totherarf
text:
When I was an apprentice we did some Appliance Repair (I even got a C+G for it). We were taught that the washing machines we repaired ..... or more correctly took to bits and re-assembled ..... like the "Keymatic Washing Machine" were about the same complexity as the NASA Moon Shot (!978). 

The difference was not in complexity but number of systems. 

Of course things are different now, with microchips potted in for pennies, but the basic premise still stands. Stuff is simple! especially if you break it down into its parts!

author: @toml.8210
text:
I use the same simple setup for my soldering station:  a plastic box with a simple soldering iron holder, a switch and an electric cord.
Later, I added a circuit breaker and outlet for testing new projects, like an adjustable DC power supply, or guitar amplifier I designed & built.

author: @TuttleScott
text: I just bought a heated vest to wear in the workshop during winter I saw cheap on the amazon to try out. Its just a usb battery bank, a cpu with mosfet that does some pwm and carbon fiber heating elements.

author: @toml.8210
text:
If people want to add fiddly bits it's because the are Safety Sallys and have no idea how simple the thing really is, so they need to complicate it to make up for their own faults or inability to use a heater safely.
  The same people probably think a terracotta pot heater is safe even with 8 tea candles!  Or they think tap water is an electrical conductor.
